title: Workers Around the World Say 'No' to Paying for War
summary: Have you noticed things cost more lately? Maybe your favorite snack or a trip to the movies? Around the world, workers are feeling the pinch too — and they're blaming a war far away. Friday was May Day, a special day in many countries to celebrate workers. But this year, people weren't just celebrating. They were protesting! From Paris to Manila, crowds marched with signs demanding higher wages and peace. Why? Because the war in Iran is making energy and food prices soar. In France, unions shouted "bread, peace and freedom." In the Philippines, protesters near the U.S. Embassy held banners saying "no troops, no bases, no war games." Some even clashed with police. One labor leader said, "Every Filipino worker now knows that the situation here is deeply connected to the global crisis." In Indonesia, the president joined a huge rally. Workers asked for help as prices rise and materials get hard to find. In Pakistan, a construction worker named Mohammad Maskeen, 55, said simply: "How will I bring vegetables and other necessities home if I don't work?" In Turkey, police arrested some protesters who tried to march in a famous square. In Italy, the government gave nearly a billion euros in job help, but critics called it "pure propaganda." In Portugal, workers are still fighting over new labor laws they say will hurt their rights. And in France, there was a big debate about whether people should be allowed to work on May Day — the one day almost everyone has off. This year, May Day wasn't just a holiday. It was a loud message: workers don't want to pay the price for war.
why_it_matters: This shows that when countries far away are at war, it can make things you buy more expensive, so peace helps your family's budget.

keywords.explanation: showing publicly that you disagree with something, often by marching with signs
the money a worker earns for doing a job
to increase very quickly and strongly, like a bird flying high
organized groups of workers who join together to ask for better pay and conditions
the official building of one country's government in another country
basic things you need to live, like food and shelter

questions.question: What are workers blaming for rising prices?
In which country did protesters clash with police near the U.S. Embassy?
What did Italy's government give nearly a billion euros for?
What was the big debate in France about?
options: whether people should be allowed to work on May Day
whether to join the war in Iran
whether to ban May Day parades
questions.correct_answer: whether people should be allowed to work on May Day

background_read: May Day, or International Workers' Day on May 1, honors workers and their rights. It is celebrated in many countries around the world.
In the past, May Day has often been a day for protests as well as celebration.
An embassy is a country's official office in a foreign nation, where diplomats work.
When wars happen, the cost of things like fuel and food often goes up, hitting workers and families hardest.
Article_Structure: WHO: Workers, unions, and protesters in many countries like France, Philippines, Indonesia, Pakistan, Turkey, Italy, Portugal.
WHAT: On May Day, they marched and protested, demanding higher wages and peace because of rising prices.
WHERE: Around the world, from Paris to Manila, and in many other countries.
WHY: The war in Iran has made energy and food prices soar, making life harder for workers.

perspectives.description: They believe that the war is causing unfair price hikes, and they demand better wages to afford basic things.
They think that economic support, like Italy's job help, is enough, but critics call it propaganda — they want real change, not just short-term fixes.
May Day has always been a day when workers voice their concerns, and this year's protests show that global conflicts directly affect ordinary people everywhere.
